# Challenge
n = 
e = 3
c = 265750796544524813279593212432449196615079221336691322757789112300992416544443199327878795838656718008585379499164951459714550098017080243141448046859054840646315837117021783329125

# Walkthrough
## Initial Assessment
Based on our variables and the challenge name, it is clear that this is a RSA challenge.
One thing to always try on RSA problems is to see if n is factorable using factorDB, however based on how long it is on this problem, we will quickly find that it isn't factorable.

The thing that should stand out here in this problem is our e value. Usually e = 65537, but now it is 3, which means is could potentially be vulnerable to RSA Low Public Exponent Attack.

## Problem Solving
To test this attack, we need to find the cube root of our ciphertext. 
Python pow unfortunately loses precision when taking float exponents, so pow (c, 1/3) isn't going to do the trick.

As shown in the walkthrough, sympy.integer_nth_root is going to be a great workaround.

After taking the cube root of the ciphertext and converting to byte format, we find the answer

## Solution
flag{Low_Public_Exponent}
